1_access_authorization.heading: 1. Access and Authorization
1_access_authorization.body: This section verifies that access to the DUT is restricted to authorized entities using secure communication mechanisms.

2_cryptographic_communication.heading: 2. Cryptographic Based Secure Communication
2_cryptographic_communication.body: The DUT shall protect data in transit using industry-standard and NIST-recommended cryptographic protocols and algorithms.

3_requirement_description.heading: 3. Requirement Description
3_requirement_description.body: Secure communication mechanism between the Network product and the connected entities shall use only the industry standard and NIST recommended cryptographic protocols such as IPSEC, VPN, SSH, TLS/SSL, etc. Also, Network product shall provide all cryptographic service such as encryption, decryption, key exchange, authentication, data integrity etc. using the industry accepted and NIST recommended cryptographic algorithms (with standard key lengths) such as SHA, Diffie-Hellman, AES, RSA etc

sub_4_1.heading: 4.1 Verification of Supported Communication Protocols
sub_4_1.body: Nmap scanning was performed to identify the communication protocols and services supported by the DUT.

sub_4_2.heading: 4.2 SSH Host Key Configuration
sub_4_2.body: The client was configured to store the DUT's SSH host key in the known_hosts file, ensuring mutual authentication during SSH connection establishment.

5_preconditions.heading: 5. Preconditions
bullets: OEM should provide the list of entities that connect to DUT through which interfaces and the connection protocols between them.

Network product documentation stating which security protocols for protection of data in transit are implemented and which profiles in TS 33.310 and TS 33.210 are applicable is provided by the vendor.

6_test_objective.heading: 6. Test Objective
6_test_objective.body: To verify if the DUT management traffic shall be protected strictly using secure cryptographic controls prescribed in Table 1 of the latest document “Cryptographic Controls For Indian Telecom Security Assurance Requirements (ITSAR)”.

sub_7_4.heading: 7.4 Test Execution Steps
bullets: The tester shall establish a secure connection between the network product and the peer and verify that all protocol versions and combinations of cryptographic algorithms that are mandated by the security profile are supported by the network product, using a packet capturing tool (e.g., Wireshark).
The tester should also try to attempt to connect to DUT using a deliberately unsupported cipher to check if connection to DUT is possible or not.

8_expected_results.heading: 8. Expected Results for Pass
8_expected_results.body: Handshake of the selected management protocol is completed between DUT and Tester device and data exchanged between DUT and Tester device is encrypted using the selected encryption algorithm.

tc1.heading: 9.1 Test Case Number: 1
tc1.name: TC1_DUT_SUPPORTS_SECURE_CIPHERS
tc1.steps_prefix: The tester should run the command
tc1.steps_suffix: and check for any unsupported cipher.
tc1.screenshot_title: Test Case 1 : Executed cmd output: sudo nmap --script ssh2-enum-algos

tc2.heading: 9.2 Test Case Number: 2
tc2.name: TC1_PROTECT_DATA_INFO_TRANSFER_USING_SSH
tc2.desc: DUT and tester device should support traffic protection through SSH only through provided cryptographic methods
steps_bullets: The tester should attempt to ssh into the DUT and capture the SSH traffic using wireshark
Analyze the ssh handshake in the p-cap file.
tc2.sub_9_2_1: 9.2.1 Security Ciphers Utilized During Data Transmission (Verbose Fetch)
screenshot_titles: Test Case 2 : SSH CLI Packet Capture showing SSH Handshake Traffic
Test Case 2 : SSH Handshake showing {kex_label} Key Exchange Algorithm : {kex_algo}
Test Case 2 : SSH Data Encryption using {enc_label} Cipher : {cipher_algo}
screenshot_overviews: The above screenshot shows the command line packet capture using t-shark while performing SSH communication between the tester system and the DUT. The captured packets include the SSH handshake packets exchanged during the establishment of the SSH session.

The above screenshot shows the SSH handshake process where the Key Exchange algorithm {kex_algo} is used to securely establish the cryptographic session between the SSH client and server. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, this key exchange algorithm is classified as {kex_label}.

The above screenshot shows the encrypted SSH communication packets after the successful handshake process. The Encryption algorithm {cipher_algo} is used to encrypt the SSH data packets exchanged between the client and the DUT. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, this cipher is classified as {enc_label}.

overview_templates.cipher: The above screenshot shows the SSH negotiation attempt where the weak encryption algorithm {algo} was forced during the SSH communication between the tester system and the DUT. During the SSH handshake process, the client attempted to negotiate this cipher with the server. The result of the negotiation shows that the algorithm was {negotiation_text}. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, the algorithm {algo} is classified as {status_label}.
overview_templates.mac: The above screenshot shows the SSH negotiation attempt where the weak Message Authentication Code (MAC) algorithm {algo} was forced during the SSH communication. The SSH client attempted to use this MAC algorithm while establishing the encrypted session with the DUT. The negotiation result indicates that the algorithm was {negotiation_text}. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, the MAC algorithm {algo} is classified as {status_label}.
overview_templates.kex: The above screenshot shows the SSH handshake process where the weak Key Exchange algorithm {algo} was attempted during the establishment of the SSH session. The client attempted to negotiate this key exchange algorithm with the DUT. The negotiation result shows that the algorithm was {negotiation_text}. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, the key exchange algorithm {algo} is classified as {status_label}.
overview_templates.host_key: The above screenshot shows the SSH handshake process where the weak host key algorithm {algo} was attempted during the authentication phase of the SSH communication. The client attempted to negotiate this host key algorithm with the DUT. The negotiation result indicates that the algorithm was {negotiation_text}. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, the host key algorithm {algo} is classified as {status_label}.
overview_templates.default: The above screenshot shows the SSH negotiation attempt using the algorithm {algo}. The negotiation result indicates that the algorithm was {negotiation_text}. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, this algorithm is classified as {status_label}.

10_ssh_observation.heading: 10. Test Observation for SSH
10_ssh_observation.pass_text: It was observed that the Device Under Test (DUT) complies with the prescribed secure cryptographic requirements. All test cases related to secure SSH cipher support, encrypted communication protection, and weak algorithm negotiation have successfully passed.
10_ssh_observation.fail_prefix: It was observed that the Device Under Test (DUT) does not fully comply with the prescribed secure cryptographic requirements, as one or more security validation test cases have failed. The failure was identified in the following test case(s):
10_ssh_observation.fail_suffix: . This indicates that the DUT permits insecure cryptographic configurations which may weaken the security of SSH communication.

tc1.name: TC1_DUT_SUPPORTS_SECURE_CIPHERS
tc1.pass_remark: All cryptographic algorithms comply with policy.
tc1.fail_remark: One or more weak cryptographic algorithms detected.

tc2.name: TC2_PROTECT_DATA_INFO_TRANSFER_USING_SSH_PART_1
tc2.pass_remark: SSH communication is protected using secure cryptographic methods.
tc2.fail_remark: SSH communication used weak or unsupported cryptographic methods.

tc3.name: TC2_SSH_WEAK_ALGORITHM_NEGOTIATION_PART_2
tc3.pass_remark: The DUT correctly rejects weak SSH algorithms during negotiation.
tc3.fail_remark_prefix: The DUT allows negotiation of weak SSH algorithms:

tc1.heading: 12.1 Test Case Number: 1
tc1.name: TC1_HTTPS_CRYPTO_HARDENING
tc1.desc: DUT should support only strong TLS cryptographic ciphers for HTTPS communication
tc1.steps_prefix: The tester should run the command
tc1.steps_suffix: to enumerate the TLS ciphers supported by the DUT.
steps_bullets: Validate that only strong TLS ciphers are supported.
tc1.screenshot_title: Test Case 1 : Executed cmd output: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443

tc2.heading: 12.2 Test Case Number: 2
tc2.name: TC2_PROTECT_DATA_INFO_TRANSFER_USING_HTTPS
tc2.desc: DUT and tester devices should support traffic protection through HTTPS using secure TLS cryptographic protocols.
steps_bullets: The tester should initiate HTTPS communication with the DUT using openssl.
Capture the TLS handshake packets using t-shark.
Analyze the Server Hello packet in the captured pcap file to identify the TLS protocol version and cipher used during communication.
tc2.sub_12_2_1: 12.2.1 Security Ciphers Utilized During HTTPS Data Transmission
screenshot_titles: Test Case 2 : HTTPS CLI Packet Capture showing TLS Handshake Traffic
Test Case 2 : TLS Server Hello showing {cipher_label} Cipher : {cipher}
screenshot_overviews: The above screenshot shows the command line packet capture using t-shark while performing HTTPS communication between the tester system and the DUT. The captured packets include the TLS handshake packets exchanged during the establishment of the secure HTTPS session.

The above screenshot shows the TLS Server Hello packet captured during the HTTPS handshake process. The Server Hello message confirms that the TLS protocol version {protocol} and the cipher suite {cipher} are used to secure the communication between the tester system and the DUT. According to the cryptographic ITSAR guidelines, the cipher {cipher} is classified as {cipher_label}.

13_https_observation.heading: 13. Test Observation for HTTPS
13_https_observation.pass_text: It was observed that the Device Under Test (DUT) complies with the prescribed secure cryptographic requirements for HTTPS communication. All test cases related to secure TLS cipher support and encrypted HTTPS communication protection have successfully passed.
13_https_observation.fail_prefix: It was observed that the Device Under Test (DUT) does not fully comply with the prescribed secure cryptographic requirements for HTTPS communication, as one or more security validation test cases have failed. The failure was identified in the following test case(s):
13_https_observation.fail_suffix: . This indicates that the DUT permits insecure TLS protocol versions or cryptographic cipher configurations which may weaken the security of HTTPS communication.

tc1.name: TC1_HTTPS_CRYPTO_HARDENING
tc1.pass_remark: HTTPS service supports only secure TLS cryptographic algorithms.
tc1.fail_remark: Weak or unsupported TLS cryptographic algorithms detected.

tc2.name: TC2_PROTECT_DATA_INFO_TRANSFER_USING_HTTPS
tc2.pass_remark: HTTPS communication is protected using secure TLS protocol and cipher.
tc2.fail_remark: HTTPS communication used weak or unsupported TLS protocol or cipher.
